questions.scenario: A deny rule at priority 200 and an allow rule at priority 1000 both match the same traffic. Which one wins?

options.text: The allow rule because allow always wins.
Whichever rule was created most recently.
The rule with the longer name.

questions.explanation: Lower priority numbers are evaluated first, so priority 200 wins before priority 1000.

questions.scenario: A reviewer sees a broad allow at priority 1000. What should they check next?

options.text: Whether any lower-numbered matching rule already denies or allows the traffic.
Whether the rule name is alphabetical.
Whether the project has more labels.
Whether the subnet description mentions security.

questions.explanation: Priority is part of the decision. A visible allow may not be the rule that wins.

questions.scenario: Which practices make firewall priority easier to review?

options.text: Use clear priority bands for broad denies and narrow service allows.
Document why a rule sits above another matching rule.
Avoid random priority numbers when a team convention exists.
Assume priority does not matter if the rule exists.

questions.explanation: Priority conventions make rule conflicts reviewable.
